rules_fired.because: Thiol-based reducing agents (DTT, β-mercaptoethanol) are rapidly oxidised by dissolved O₂ in aqueous buffers. At 4°C in standard buffer, DTT has an effective half-life of approximately 1 week; at room temperature in air-exposed buffer, this drops to hours. β-mercaptoethanol oxidises at comparable rates and also volatilises, making fresh preparation doubly important. For critical applications (disulfide bond reduction, enzyme protection), the stock should be made fresh on the day of use. Note: phosphine-based reducing agents (TCEP) are air-stable and are excluded from this rule — see tcep_air_stable.
rules_fired.cite: Cleland WW (1964) Biochemistry 3:480–482 (original DTT paper, redox characterisation). Sigma-Aldrich DTT Product Information Sheet D9779.

rules_fired.because: Skin-penetrant reagents cross intact skin and can carry dissolved solutes transdermally. DMSO is the canonical example — contact with a DMSO drug stock is a real toxicity exposure route even through nitrile gloves with extended contact. 2-mercaptoethanol and acrylamide are similarly absorbed through skin. Nitrile gloves (not latex) are required; change gloves immediately after any spill; never work with these without gloves.
rules_fired.cite: NIOSH skin absorption hazard notes. Braue EH et al. (1999) Occup Environ Med: percutaneous absorption review.

rules_fired.because: The SDS (Section 7 or 8) specifies ventilation controls or fume hood use for this reagent. This category spans volatile toxic solvents (pyridine, DMF, heptane), cytotoxic and genotoxic compounds (docetaxel, streptozocin, azoxymethane), and reagents whose vapours or aerosols pose an inhalation hazard. A chemical fume hood provides the primary engineering control — never handle these reagents on the open bench.
rules_fired.cite: OSHA Laboratory Standard 29 CFR 1910.1450. Manufacturer SDS Section 7/8.
